name: M.S. in Occupational Safety and Health Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 23 surfaced courses cover the OSHE domain comprehensively and logically: hazard recognition and control (IE 615 Industrial Hygiene, IE 614 Safety Engineering Methods), systems safety (IE 685), ergonomics and human factors (IE 661/664/665, IE 669), toxicology and risk (EVSC 616, EVSC 614), legal aspects (EM 633, IE 608), and a culminating project/independent-research path (IE 700B, IE 725). Short prerequisite chains such as IE 614 into IE 675 show intended progression from foundations to specialization.
dimensions.suggestion: Group the pool explicitly into required core versus elective concentrations (safety, industrial hygiene, ergonomics) so the intended pathway through the 36 credits is unambiguous.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 1, so gating exists (e.g., IE 675 requires IE 614; IE 665 requires IE 355 or IE 699) but is shallow for a 36-credit professional degree; missingCourseCount is 2 (IE 681 and IE 700 dangling), which are minor code issues against an otherwise clean set.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the IE 681 and IE 700 references and deepen a couple of chains (e.g., statistics into risk-assessment and systems-safety courses) so sequencing is clearer.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes are present in the data; despite a rich course pool, the page states no explicit competencies (hazard anticipation, exposure assessment, safety-program management) that graduates should attain.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 program learning outcomes aligned to recognized OSH competency frameworks (e.g., anticipate, recognize, evaluate, and control workplace hazards).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is 36, transparently stated and appropriately above the 30-credit norm for this professionally oriented, NIOSH-affiliated degree (note IE 682 references a trainee scholarship), but creditReconcileDelta is null and no core/elective credit split is shown.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the plan of study specifying how the 36 credits divide among required core, technical electives, and the project/research requirement.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The entry is rich and readable: 23 courses with substantive, specific descriptions and stated credits, and clean titles across IE, EVSC, EM, BME, HRM, and ME prefixes. The main gap is the absence of a parsed plan of study or labeled core/elective terms (requirementGroups is 0).
dimensions.suggestion: Add a visible plan-of-study structure (core vs. elective groupings and any recommended sequence) to complement the strong course descriptions.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum closely tracks contemporary OSH practice: industrial hygiene (IE 615), systems safety (IE 685), risk assessment (EVSC 614), toxicology (EVSC 616), HAZWOPER (EVSC 603), cognitive engineering (IE 662), and legal/product-liability topics (EM 633, IE 608), reflecting the multidisciplinary reality of the profession. Notable gaps are formal process-safety management, safety data analytics, and Total Worker Health.
dimensions.suggestion: Add electives in process safety management and safety data analytics to close the most visible currency gaps.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are consistently specific and informative, for example IE 615 detailing recognition/evaluation/control of noise, heat, chemical, and radiation exposures, and IE 685 covering system-safety planning and management; the pool is largely free of thin placeholders.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are frequently stated but as prose that leans on undergraduate references (e.g., 'IE 355 or equivalent', 'one year of college physics and one semester of college chemistry or biology'), and a few are vague ('human factors engineering'); two dangling references (IE 681, IE 700) slightly reduce consistency.

missingTopics.rationale: Chemical/process safety and hazard analysis (PHA, LOPA) is a core professional area only partially touched by IE 685 and IE 614.
No course applies statistics/ML to incident and exposure data despite IE 604 being available in the department.
Quantifying exposure-disease relationships complements the toxicology (EVSC 616) and risk (EVSC 614) courses but is not represented.
A high-hazard, heavily regulated sector is not addressed as a dedicated course in the visible list.
Beyond IE 614's fire-protection mention, a dedicated fire-safety/emergency-preparedness course is missing.

emergingTrends.trend: Wearable sensors and real-time exposure monitoring
emergingTrends.rationale: IoT-based dosimetry and physiological monitoring are transforming industrial hygiene practice and extend IE 615.
Machine-learning models for incident prediction and leading-indicator analytics are entering safety management.

emergingTrends.trend: Total Worker Health and psychosocial hazards
emergingTrends.rationale: NIOSH's integration of well-being, stress, and ergonomics broadens the field beyond physical hazards, extending IE 661/664.
Safe deployment of collaborative robots (cobots) is a growing occupational-safety concern relevant to the ergonomics strand.
Designing out hazards early is a rising professional emphasis that builds directly on IE 675 Safety in Facility and Product Design.

topSuggestions: Publish 3-5 program learning outcomes tied to recognized OSH competencies (anticipate, recognize, evaluate, control).
Group the 23-course pool into explicit required-core and elective concentrations and show the 36-credit plan of study.
Add a process safety management elective and a safety data-analytics elective to close the clearest currency gaps.
Resolve the dangling IE 681 and IE 700 references and standardize prerequisite wording.
Introduce modern content on wearable exposure monitoring and predictive safety analytics.
